event: 57 years old
male
recently diagnosed with lepromatous leprosy
on treatment (rifampicin/clofazimine/dapsone) for 2 months
presented to the hospital
complaints of abdominal distension
complaints of constipation
complaints of vomiting
complaints of 10-kg weight loss
on examination, the patient was vitally stable
evidence of peripheral lymphadenopathy
distended abdomen
positive shifting dullness
computed tomography scan of his abdomen showed mural thickening of the terminal ileum
computed tomography scan of his abdomen showed significantly enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes
computed tomography scan of his abdomen showed mesenteric fat stranding
computed tomography scan of his abdomen showed intra-abdominal free fluid
abdominal paracentesis was done
atypically large lymphocytes were found
subsequent flow cytometry showed an abnormal CD4/CD8 double-negative T-cell population (38%)
subsequent flow cytometry showed multiple phenotypic aberrancies
a cervical lymph node biopsy confirmed high-grade peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL), not other specified
a bone marrow examination showed no involvement of T-cell NHL
staged as a stage IV lymphoma
started on dexamethasone with tumor-lysis syndrome precautions
deteriorated clinically
required transfer to the medical intensive care unit (ICU) for severe sepsis
required antibiotics and antifungals
stayed in ICU care for around 1 week
once recovered, he was transferred to the national cancer center

commenced on the EPOCH chemotherapy protocol (etoposide, prednisone, vincristine sulfate [oncovin], cyclophosphamide, and doxorubicin hydrochloride [hydroxydaunorubicin]) for six cycles, along with CNS prophylaxis (intrathecal methotrexate)
attained complete metabolic remission by positron emission tomography/computed tomography
hospital course was complicated by multiple febrile neutropenia episodes
hospital course was complicated by recurrent bacteremia
developed generalized weakness without sensory changes and no clear fatigability
power was decreased in all proximal and distal muscles (3/5)
differential diagnosis included critical illness myopathy-neuropathy, toxic neuropathy, and paraneoplastic syndrome
a complete neurophysiological electromyogram/nerve conduction study was performed
showed normal distal latencies
showed normal compound muscle action potential
showed normal conduction velocities along with normal F waves in all motor nerves studied
sensory nerve studies revealed normal onset latencies
sensory nerve studies revealed normal sensory nerve action potential amplitude
sensory nerve studies revealed normal conduction velocities
needle electromyogram of proximal and distal muscles in the lower and upper limbs exhibited normal insertional activity
needle electromyogram of proximal and distal muscles in the lower and upper limbs exhibited no spontaneous activity
needle electromyogram of proximal and distal muscles in the lower and upper limbs exhibited normal motor unit action potential
needle electromyogram of proximal and distal muscles in the lower and upper limbs exhibited poor recruitment effects
repetitive nerve stimulation (at 30–40 Hz) showed a significant incremental response
suggesting a presynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder (likely LEMS)
VGCC antibodies were requested
was not done because of technical issues
started empirically on intravenous immunoglobulins for 5 days as a case of LEMS
resulted in significant improvement of his motor function
could ambulate afterward, showing a dramatic response to treatment
planned for consolidation by autologous bone marrow transplant
re-admitted to the medical ICU for severe sepsis and multiorgan failure
passed away around 6 months after his initial diagnosis with NHL
despite maintaining a remission status
